speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. Today is November 15, 1996. I am interviewing Margaret Lowe, and my name is Judith [INAUDIBLE]. OK, Margaret, you were telling me about the day the war ended and how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, it was exciting. And then, I guess everybody decided to move on with their lives. And my uncle in-- who was by then in America, started urging me to come to America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was loath to leave England, you know? I didn't want to really leave. And-- but I moved away from the war work and I moved to-- to-- back to London, to these people, Gunters, and I went back into millinery. And-- and I guess Ken came back from the-- from the army, from overseas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had been-- oh, I-- I should say that his name was originally Kurt Lowenstein. And in the army-- the British Army did not naturalize, as American Army did, so they encouraged to change their names so they couldn't be traced to be German Jews if they were taken prisoner. So he changed it to Kenneth Lowe. So when I first knew him as Kurt, but afterwards got used to call him Ken. And he came back from his overseas service and lived outside of London. And we started dating or seeing each other. And eventually, my visas came through and everything else and-- I left for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself with-- you know, on a-- on a ship. And Ken-- we had-- we had sort of lost a little touch, because we had had a date and I couldn't make it and had no telephones. And when I wrote him a letter of apology-- anyways, we lost touch a little bit, and he went to Winnipeg, where his brother was living in Canada. So we lost touch with each other. And I came to America, and I was not overjoyed to be here. My uncle, at that time, his wife was dying, which I didn't know. And I didn't know them because they had lived in Berlin, and so I didn't feel terribly comfortable. And of course, I didn't know-- they didn't tell me about how sick the aunt really was. And they rented a room for me, and I was pretty lonesome here. And also, being uprooted twice, I really didn't fit into-- to America at all. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I had different jobs. I first worked for-- at Saks Fifth Avenue as a saleswoman. And millinery was immediately not successful, because through my cousin I got a job in millinery and they let me go after two hours or something. Just they didn't show me there were irons that were-- steam irons that were attached to the ceiling with thing, and nobody was allowed to talk. Nobody helped each other, and I just didn't know what was going on. And so I was quite happy to work at Saks for a while, and later I had other jobs. And-- and by a coincidence, my husband-- I mean, not my husband, but Ken, had at that time come to New York to visit a cousin of his, and by coincidence met one of the women that were in our youth group in Birmingham. She lived in Washington Heights, and she had my-- also, I was not in touch with her at all-- she had my uncle's address, which, of course, reached me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- when he got back to Winnipeg, we started corresponding, and eventually he came-- after two years, I guess, he came to America. And I guess it was another two years before we got married. We knew each other all together 10 years before we got married. And-- and we got married on December-- no, we were supposed to get married in December, and my husband fell ill at the time. And we postponed it till the 11th of January, 1953. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what else? And then I had-- well, I had a son, just one son, and he's born-- David. And he's born on the 5th of March, 1955. And we lived at that time in Forest Hills, and my husband was a plumber. He stayed with his profession.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after David was born, we wanted to have a house. And my husband-- we bought land, and he-- partly, he had it contracted, but a lot of the work, every brick of it he did himself-- this house that I still live in. And when David was two years old, we-- we moved to Merrick, to my present house, and-- we-- where I still live. And my husband, unfortunately, after-- we had 40 good years, but he died two years ago, in May 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And my son [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lives in Boston now. When he-- after he finished college, he joined the Peace Corps, and he lived in-- he worked in Kenya for two years. And then he came back here and continued his studies, and again went back to Kenya for some more years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now lives--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now he lives in Boston. He has a job that he likes, and unfortunately, isn't married yet. [LAUGHS] We hope he finds a nice girl. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to tell me about why it was important to you to give this testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well initially, I really don't think of myself as a Holocaust survivor, and I don't think even that I have a story that's worth telling. But a few years ago, there was a reunion of the Kindertransport, and that's when we all started again talking about this. It was 50 years after it all started. And it took us all these years before we reminisced about these things. And now, since they are doing these interviews, I thought it's important maybe to tell about the people who helped us. I mean the people who-- luckily, I didn't have such terrible hardships. I think it did something. It certainly did something to-- to our [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, we have a different view, maybe, than we would have had otherwise. But what's really important is to-- to tell of the people who helped along the way. And I know my uncle-- my father's brother, Oskar-- I came across letters now for doing this interview where he, to the last day in the 1940s, yet tried to help to get my parents out via Cuba, via whatever, and it just wasn't-- wasn't possible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My aunt-- my father's sister, Jda-- she immediately got us permission to emigrate to-- to Switzerland, and that also came to nothing, because my parents couldn't get the passport necessary. There was a cousin of my father's-- it was, like, a second or third generation American, and he-- his name was Leon Sweet, of Salt Lake City, Utah, and he sent us affidavits, which my parents could not make use of. But he sent me, regularly, money to support me in England, because the people I lived with could not afford to either send me to school, or even keep me because they had also lost in the Nazi time. And this Leon Sweet supported-- I understand he's a rich man. He supported 26 families in Europe during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all these people should be remembered. I had many, many very supportive friends in England. And the family Gunter, Fritz and Hilda Gunter, who were non-Jewish friends of my parents, and had me in their home all the years of the war and treated me like their daughter. And other, you know, other-- many non-Jewish families in England, friends of-- I mean, girls I worked with, their parents took me into their home and always were very supportive. So I feel that I personally haven't had terrible hardships, other than the loss of my parents. But it did something, I guess, also to my-- to my outlook on life. I feel I have nowhere-- we always felt nowhere to belong. I mean, I don't have an allegiance, patriotic feelings. It's just I've been uprooted too many times, because after leaving England, here was, again, an uprooting experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, of course, lost opportunity for an education, and we lost our youth. That, I think, is the main thing, that we-- we never had a youth. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would want to add before we stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. I think I said all there is to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you please tell me about the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather's house in Oberdorf, in Wurttemberg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's-- my father's father, Zacharias Pappenheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture we took about four years ago, when we visited the town for the first time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell me about these pictures, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my grandparents on my father's side, Pappenheimer-- Zacharias and Yette. Yette, nee [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Levinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, what did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Levinger, my grandparents-- my grandmother's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Will you tell me  about these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my grandparents' gravestones in the Jewish cemetery in Oberdorf. We visited there four years ago. There is also a plaque at the cemetery wall, mentioning all the Jews from that town that had lost their lives in the Holocaust. And my parents were not on it-- on that wall plaque, but I took up a correspondence with the authorities in Germany, and they have since added my parents' name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell me about these pictures, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my maternal grandparents, Bernhardt and Sophie Gutmann. From [INAUDIBLE], also in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my parents' wedding picture in 1921. On the bottom row, on the left-hand side, was my uncle Jacob Goldmeier. The next gentleman, I don't know. The next person was my grandmother on my father's side, then my mother and my father. And the next lady is my grandmother and grandfather on my mother's side. And the back row, from the left, is my mother's sister, Jenny; my father's brother-in-law, Sigmund, and his wife, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Minzl, Mina. Then, on the other side of my father is my mother's brother, Paul, my father's brother, Oskar, and my father's brother-in-law and his-- William and his wife, which is my father's sister, Jda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell me about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father as a child, on the right-hand side, and with his three siblings. From left, it is my father's younger brother Oskar, and his sister, Jda, I believe, then my father. And at the bottom is the other sister, Minzl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father on the left, and his brother Oskar as soldiers in World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the German Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the German Army, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a group picture of the German Army hospital, and my father is on the bottom left, with-- with his nose and his arm bandaged after receiving injuries. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this medal, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's Iron Cross that he received in the First World War. On Veterans Day, on the 11th of November, they used to march, you know, the veterans organizations, and my-- my father never wanted to put-- put it on. My mother always urged him to. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was because of this cross that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, as being a front line soldier, we were spared a little longer, although it didn't help us in the long run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me about the picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a war hospital in the First World War, and my mother is a nurse on the-- the third, in the rear, from the left. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house where I lived when I-- in-- in Germany, in Munich. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The apartment house that--

speaker: interviewee
text: The apartment house, and I lived in-- on the first floor of this house till I left Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my parents and me in Munich, and I think I was six or eight years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Fritz Gunter, who was a gentleman and his wife who took me in in England, after I left Germany and were friends of my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Hilda Gunter, and I lived with them in England. When-- when her husband was interned, she asked me to ask-- call her Aunt Hilda. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture, taken January 11, 1953. And the man standing to the left of me is the rabbi, who was a rabbi in Munich, Dr. Leo [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me about the picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Kurt Lowenstein, when he joined the army. And he later became my husband, and he changed his name in the army to Kenneth Lowe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my husband and myself and it was taken at a friend's house about five or six years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son, David, and I guess it was taken about two years ago. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, please tell me what this document is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is called fuhrungszeugnis. It's-- it's from the police in Munich sent for my emigration, certifying that I had lived in-- in Munich from 1922 till I emigrated in 1939. What is interesting on it is that you see after my name Margarete, there is Sara Pappenheimer. At that time, the Jews were obliged-- all Jewish women had to take, as a second name, Sara, and all Jewish men had to take, I think, as their second name, Israel, I believe. And it was never my-- my name, actually. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the document, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an affidavit that was issued to my parents and myself by Leon Sweet-- gave a guarantee for us to emigrate to the United States. We were never able to use it, as our number was too high, and my parents perished before-- before our numbers came up. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me this document, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a document that I received after the war, stating that my pants were deported on the 20th of November, 1941 to Riga, and never survived.
